question: The core marketing metrics every manager should understand include: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC), Customer Lifetime Value (LTV), and Churn Rate. How are these calculated and why do they matter together?
options: CAC, LTV, and Churn are independent metrics with no analytical relationship

They form a coherent framework for evaluating customer economics: CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost) = Total Sales & Marketing Spend / Number of New Customers Acquired; if Spotify spends $500M on marketing in a quarter and acquires 10M new subscribers, CAC = $50/subscriber. LTV (Customer Lifetime Value) = Average Revenue per Customer × Gross Margin × Average Customer Lifespan; for Spotify Premium ($10/month × 12 months = $120/year × 68% gross margin = $82/year × 3 years average) = $246 LTV. Churn Rate = Customers who stopped in a period / Total customers at start of period; if 1,000 Spotify subscribers started the month and 20 cancelled, monthly churn = 2%. The relationship: LTV/CAC ratio: Spotify's LTV ($246) / CAC ($50) = 4.9× — for every $1 spent acquiring a customer, the customer generates $4.9 in value over their lifetime; the benchmark: LTV/CAC > 3× is generally considered a healthy growth business; < 1× means the company is destroying value by growing (acquiring customers costs more than they're worth); the churn-LTV link: monthly churn of 2% = average customer lifespan of 50 months; if churn improved to 1% = 100 months; the same $10/month customer is worth 2× as much with half the churn — making retention as important as acquisition
CAC is only relevant for subscription businesses
LTV calculation requires a specialized financial background
explanation: Unit economics: the LTV/CAC framework is called 'unit economics' — the economics of each individual customer transaction, as opposed to aggregate company financials. A business can have impressive top-line revenue growth while having terrible unit economics (acquiring customers at more than they're worth). WeWork's unit economics: WeWork's per-location economics were negative (each location lost money the longer it was open due to lease commitments vs. variable occupancy revenue); the company grew rapidly (revenue growth disguised the unit economics problem); the S-1 filing revealed the unit economics, triggering the canceled IPO. The Payback Period additional metric: CAC Payback Period = CAC / (Monthly Revenue per Customer × Gross Margin); for Spotify: $50 / ($10 × 68%) = 7.4 months. The CAC payback answers 'how long until the customer pays back their acquisition cost?' — important for cash flow planning; a business with 18-month CAC payback must fund those 18 months of customer development before receiving the cash back.

question: Net Promoter Score (NPS) asks customers: 'How likely are you to recommend [company/product] to a friend or colleague?' on a 0-10 scale. Promoters (9-10) are subtracted from Detractors (0-6) to calculate NPS. Apple's NPS ~70; Dell's ~12; Comcast's consistently negative. What is NPS's relationship to business performance?
options: NPS is a purely academic metric with no relationship to actual business outcomes

NPS correlates with revenue growth and retention, but has significant limitations that require understanding: the NPS-growth relationship: Fred Reichheld (Bain, 2003) found that NPS was a strong predictor of revenue growth — companies with high NPS grew faster because: Promoters generate word-of-mouth referrals (free customer acquisition); Detractors churn and warn others away (customer acquisition cost increases); the business implication: each 1-point NPS improvement correlates with approximately 2.5% revenue growth in Reichheld's original research. Apple's NPS advantage: Apple's ~70 NPS (vs. industry average ~30) means 70% more Promoters than Detractors — customers who enthusiastically recommend Apple products to friends (significant contribution to low CAC). Comcast's negative NPS: negative means more Detractors than Promoters — customers who are actively warning others away; the fact that Comcast grew despite negative NPS reflects monopolistic market position (many Americans have only one cable provider option) — another limitation of NPS: it predicts competitive market outcomes better than monopoly market outcomes. The limitations: NPS doesn't diagnose why customers score as they do; scores vary enormously by industry (healthcare vs. consumer electronics benchmarks are incomparable); survey response rate bias (vocal critics and enthusiasts are overrepresented); asking the question ≠ acting on the answer
Higher NPS is always achieved by reducing prices
NPS is only relevant for consumer-facing businesses
explanation: NPS in practice: the metric's power is in its simplicity (one question, one number that executives can track) and its action orientation (you need to understand what drives Detractors to improve). The root cause analysis: 'Why did you give us a 4/10?' reveals what to fix. Without the follow-up question, NPS is a lagging indicator not an improvement tool. The NPS criticism: Jared Spool (UX researcher) and others argue that NPS is a 'vanity metric' — teams optimize for the score rather than the underlying customer experience. Companies that obsess about NPS while customers report terrible actual experiences are missing the point. Amazon's obsession with real metrics: Amazon reportedly cares less about NPS surveys and more about actual behavioral metrics (return rate, repeat purchase rate, contact rate — how often customers need to contact customer service, which is a direct measure of product/service failure). The benchmark trap: a NPS of 45 in insurance is excellent; a NPS of 45 in consumer electronics is mediocre. Industry benchmarking is essential for NPS interpretation.

question: Airbnb's marketing spend allocation is: 40% performance marketing (Google ads, paid social), 35% brand marketing (TV, outdoor, sponsorships), 25% PR and content. A board member questions: 'Why are we spending 35% on brand marketing when we can directly measure ROI on performance marketing?' The CMO defends the brand budget. Who has the stronger argument?
options: The board member is correct that all marketing should be performance-based with measurable ROI

Both have valid points, but measurement bias toward the measurable has well-documented costs: the board member's argument: performance marketing has direct attribution (each dollar spent → specific conversions tracked); ROI is calculable; it's accountable. The CMO's argument: (1) Performance marketing efficiency is constrained by brand equity — Google search click-through rates are higher when people recognize the brand; the cost-per-click is lower for searches of 'Airbnb' vs. 'short-term rental'; brand advertising that creates 'Airbnb = travel' association in consumer minds reduces the cost of every future performance marketing impression; (2) Brand advertising builds long-term demand that doesn't appear in 30-day attribution windows — a consumer who sees an Airbnb TV ad in March might not book until September; the September booking's attribution goes to the search ad, not the March TV ad; (3) Airbnb's actual experiment: in 2020, Airbnb cut all performance marketing during COVID (budget necessity); brand direct traffic (people searching 'airbnb.com' directly) remained high, proving brand equity existed independent of performance spending; (4) Competitor context: if Airbnb eliminates brand marketing, Booking.com and VRBO maintain their brand investment; Airbnb cedes share-of-mind. The integrated measurement solution: media mix modeling (MMM) attributes revenue across all channels including brand — more sophisticated than last-click attribution alone
All marketing budget should be allocated exclusively to performance-based advertising
Brand marketing effectiveness can never be measured and should be eliminated
explanation: Marketing attribution debate: this is the most important current debate in marketing. The 'measurability trap': digital marketing tools (Google Analytics, Facebook Ads Manager) make performance marketing easy to measure and attribute; brand marketing (TV, outdoor, audio) is harder to attribute to specific purchases. This creates systematic organizational bias toward performance marketing (it's measurable, accountable) at the expense of brand marketing (it's hard to attribute, but may create the brand equity that makes performance marketing efficient). Airbnb's COVID experiment: Airbnb stopped all performance marketing in Q2 2020 (COVID travel halt); direct bookings recovered to 80% of pre-COVID levels by end of 2020 — demonstrating that Airbnb had brand equity that operated independently of paid search. This is one of the best natural experiments in marketing because it shows what direct and brand-driven demand looks like without performance marketing support. The Media Mix Modeling approach: econometric models that analyze the relationship between marketing spend (across all channels) and revenue over time, controlling for seasonality, economic conditions, and pricing — provide holistic attribution that includes brand marketing's contribution. P&G, Coca-Cola, and Unilever use MMM as their primary marketing measurement framework.

question: A D2C (direct-to-consumer) fashion brand has the following metrics: CAC = $80, Average Order Value = $60, Orders per customer per year = 2, Gross Margin = 55%, Churn (annual) = 45%. Is this business viable? What specific metric must improve for the business to be sustainable?
options: Revenue growth rate is the only metric needed to assess D2C business viability

Unit economics analysis reveals a structurally unprofitable business that requires either CAC reduction or retention improvement: the unit economics calculation: Annual revenue per customer = $60 × 2 orders = $120; Gross profit per customer per year = $120 × 55% = $66; Annual churn = 45%, so average customer lifespan = 1 / 0.45 = 2.2 years; LTV = $66 × 2.2 years = $145; LTV/CAC = $145 / $80 = 1.8× — below the 3× minimum benchmark for a sustainable business; the problem specifics: the CAC ($80) is nearly recovering in the first year but the high churn (45%) means customers don't stay long enough for the ratio to become healthy; the specific metric to improve: churn; if churn fell from 45% to 25% (customer lifespan = 4 years), LTV = $66 × 4 = $264; LTV/CAC = 264/80 = 3.3× — now viable; the business alternatives: (1) Improve retention (reduce churn — email re-engagement, loyalty program, personalization); (2) Increase order frequency (from 2 to 3 orders/year, which increases LTV by 50%); (3) Reduce CAC (from $80 to $40, through referral programs, organic social, PR); (4) Raise gross margin (better sourcing or pricing — from 55% to 65%)
D2C businesses always have better economics than wholesale fashion brands
LTV/CAC ratio above 1 is always sufficient for business sustainability
explanation: D2C fashion economics: the D2C model eliminates wholesale margins (instead of selling to Nordstrom at 50% of MSRP, the brand keeps the full retail price minus fulfillment cost). This creates higher gross margins. But D2C companies must pay for their own customer acquisition (vs. wholesale where the retailer drives traffic to their store), creating the CAC burden. The D2C 'direct acquisition crisis' (2021-2022): Facebook's iOS privacy changes (ATT — App Tracking Transparency, 2021) reduced ad targeting effectiveness, causing D2C CAC to increase by 30-50% across the industry. Brands that had been marginally viable suddenly had LTV/CAC ratios below 1. Casper, Warby Parker, Allbirds, and others that had been growing at negative unit economics needed to radically improve their economics or face business model failure. The retention lever: for a brand with 45% annual churn, understanding why customers leave (price? quality? occasion-based purchase with no repeat need?) is the essential question before choosing a retention strategy. A customer who bought one dress for a specific occasion may not be a natural repeat buyer — the product category's natural repurchase frequency matters.

question: Spotify's conversion funnel: Free users (Ad-supported) = 60M, Premium subscribers = 40M. The conversion rate (free-to-premium) = 40%. A competitor (Apple Music) offers only premium subscriptions at $10.99/month. Spotify's average revenue per user (ARPU) for premium = $4.37/month (lower than Apple Music due to family plans and student discounts). What does this comparison reveal about freemium economics and competitive strategy?
options: Freemium businesses are always more profitable than premium-only businesses

Freemium creates lower ARPU but higher market share — the trade-off depends on market structure and cost economics: the comparative economics: Spotify revenue model: 40M premium × $4.37 ARPU = $174M/month; plus 60M free users generating ad revenue (modest); Apple Music: fewer total subscribers but higher ARPU (~$10/month). The freemium strategic logic: (1) Discovery mechanism: free users (40M in hypothetical) are potential premium converters; eliminating the free tier would eliminate the conversion pipeline but also reduce total costs significantly; (2) Market penetration: Spotify reaches 100M users vs. Apple Music's premium-only approach; this scale creates: network effects (friends on Spotify), data advantages (more listening data = better recommendations), artist relationships (larger listener base = better artist distribution deals); (3) The cost of free: Spotify pays music royalties for free tier usage; each stream has a cost whether the user is free or premium; the free tier costs money and requires sufficient conversion to premium to be economically rational; the competitive implication: Apple Music, backed by Apple's hardware and services revenue, can absorb lower margin on music; Spotify must make music work as a standalone business — requiring premium conversion efficiency and cost discipline
All music streaming companies use the same business model
Lower ARPU always indicates a weaker competitive position
explanation: Freemium economics: the freemium model's viability depends on: (1) The marginal cost of the free tier being low (in software, near zero; in streaming with royalties, significant); (2) A high conversion rate from free to paid; (3) Long enough free-tier engagement to build conversion intent. Spotify's actual economics: Spotify pays ~70% of revenue as royalties to labels and artists; this leaves ~30% gross margin on premium revenue. The free tier adds royalty costs with minimal revenue. Spotify's business model has historically generated thin margins (2-4% gross margin range) because of this royalty structure. Dropbox's freemium success: Dropbox (cloud storage) has near-zero marginal cost for free users (server capacity is extremely cheap per GB); a 2% conversion rate from free to paid is highly profitable. The model transfers best when the free-tier variable cost is near zero — software, cloud storage, productivity tools. It's strained when the free tier has significant variable costs (streaming royalties, physical goods). The future of Spotify's economics: Spotify's investment in podcasts and audiobooks is partly a margin strategy — podcast royalty economics are more favorable than music royalties, allowing Spotify to shift the content mix toward higher-margin inventory.

question: A retail company measures both 'same-store sales growth' (like-for-like, or LFL) and 'total revenue growth.' In Q3, total revenue grew 12% (with 3 new stores opened in the quarter) but same-store sales declined 2%. What does this performance divergence reveal about the business, and which metric better reflects operational health?
options: Total revenue growth is always a more meaningful metric than same-store sales growth

Same-store sales decline alongside total revenue growth signals that new stores are masking underlying performance deterioration: same-store sales (LFL): measures revenue growth only in stores that have been open for at least 12 months (eliminating the new store opening effect); this is the 'organic' performance metric — is the existing business growing or shrinking? A -2% LFL means: the average established store is generating less revenue than it did last year. Total revenue growth: includes the 3 new stores' revenues; the new stores boost total revenue even if they're cannibalizing existing store sales or if the chain overall is declining on a per-store basis. The diagnostic implication: if same-store sales are declining, growth through new store openings is likely: (1) Cannibalizing existing stores (new store in a similar area draws customers away from nearby existing store); (2) Expanding into lower-quality locations (opening stores #101-103 after having already opened the best 100 locations); (3) Concealing brand deterioration (the brand is less attractive to existing customers, but opening new stores hides this). The management question: is the LFL decline driven by: traffic decline (fewer customers per store), basket decline (lower spend per visit), or both? The 3 new stores' performance: are the new stores 'performing to model' (meeting the return on investment criteria that justified opening them)? If not, stop opening new stores until the underlying LFL problem is addressed
Negative same-store sales always indicate a company in financial distress
Retail metrics are only relevant for evaluating brick-and-mortar businesses
explanation: Same-store sales as the real retail health metric: this is why professional retail analysts focus on LFL/comps rather than total revenue. The Sears/Kmart example: these retailers showed positive total revenue growth through new store openings for years while experiencing sustained same-store sales declines — the expansion concealed the underlying brand deterioration. By the time the new store openings stopped (when all viable locations were taken), the same-store sales decline was already devastating. The J.C. Penney case: J.C. Penney's Ron Johnson attempted a radical retail transformation (2011-2013) that caused same-store sales to decline 25% in one year — concealing the magnitude of the failure in total revenue figures was harder when no new stores were opening. The positive LFL case: Walmart's turnaround (2015-2018) was tracked primarily through LFL improvement — from flat to +2-3% LFL growth signaled genuine operational improvement. The new store economics: opening a new store has upfront capital cost, ramp-up period (first 12 months typically below mature store revenue), and operational complexity. LFL decline while opening new stores means the company is investing capital in expansion while the existing business is deteriorating — typically a warning sign.

question: A consumer goods brand measures its 'Share of Voice' (SOV — what percentage of total industry advertising spend is the brand's) at 18%, while its market share is 12%. The rule of thumb is: SOV > market share = building brand; SOV < market share = harvesting brand. What does this metric reveal about brand strategy, and what are the trade-offs?
options: Share of Voice has no relationship to market share or brand performance

Share of Voice greater than market share signals investment in brand equity growth at cost of current profitability: the SOV-share dynamic: Brands with SOV > market share are 'investing' — spending more on advertising (relative to industry) than their current market position justifies; the investment theory: excess SOV creates brand awareness and consideration that converts to market share over 12-24 months; this is the basis of 'brand building' strategy. Brands with SOV < market share are 'harvesting' — spending below their share position; the brand coasts on existing equity; in the short term, margins improve; over time, brand equity depletes and market share falls. The 12% share / 18% SOV analysis: the brand is investing — it spends 50% more advertising relative to competitors than its market position justifies; if the theory holds: market share should grow toward 18% over 2-3 years; the trade-off: current profitability is reduced by the 'excess' advertising spend; a 12% share brand spending at 18% SOV is investing current margin for future share. The harvest strategy implication: if a brand is planning to exit a category, deliberately cutting SOV below share extracts profitability while share declines ('milking the brand') — acceptable for planned divestitures, destructive if applied to long-term brands. The strategic decision: SOV allocation should reflect the brand's long-term role in the portfolio, not just current-period profitability
Advertising spend is always proportional to market share in all consumer goods categories
Share of Voice is relevant only for brands with television advertising budgets
explanation: The SOV/market share relationship (Jones/Binet and Field): Les Binet and Peter Field's IPA (Institute of Practitioners in Advertising) dataset analysis of 1,000+ brand campaigns found that brands with sustained SOV advantage over market share consistently gained market share over time. The study quantified the rule: for every 10 points of excess SOV, a brand gains approximately 0.5% market share annually. The P&G 'invest or harvest' portfolio framework: P&G explicitly classifies brands as 'invest' (SOV > share, building long-term equity) or 'harvest' (SOV ≤ share, maximizing current profitability before divestiture). The harvest classification is a signal that a brand is being prepared for sale. Unilever's 2022 brand rationalization: Unilever's strategy to reduce from 400 to 200 brands involves explicitly harvesting the brands being divested (cutting their advertising spend) while investing in core brands. The short-term-vs-long-term tension: financial pressure consistently drives companies to harvest brands (current-period profit improvement is measurable; future share loss is a lagging consequence). This short-term optimization is documented as one of the primary causes of brand equity decline in publicly-traded companies that face quarterly earnings pressure.
